title: Getting Better Together
concept: As GAN training progresses, the generator and discriminator push each other to improve in tandem — a sharper discriminator forces more convincing fakes, and more convincing fakes force a sharper discriminator.

exercises.prompt: Recap: in the training loop, what does the generator learn from during its turn?

options.text: The discriminator's feedback on its own fakes
Real training images shown directly to it
Labels attached to the noise vector
Nothing — its weights are frozen the whole time

exercises.explain: Right — the generator only ever learns from how the discriminator judged its fakes.

cards.title: A rising tide for both sides
cards.body: After thousands of alternating turns, something interesting happens: the generator's fakes get noticeably better — and so does the discriminator's ability to catch subtler fakes. Neither side stays ahead for long.

cards.title: Co-improvement, not one-sided progress
cards.body: As training progresses, the two networks push each other upward: a discriminator that gets better at spotting fakes forces the generator to produce more convincing ones. A generator producing more convincing fakes forces the discriminator to sharpen its judgment. Round after round, both quality bars rise together.

cards.title: Cheetahs and gazelles
cards.body: Biologists describe predators and prey locked in an evolutionary arms race: faster gazelles pressure cheetahs to evolve quicker sprints, and faster cheetahs pressure gazelles to evolve quicker escapes. Neither species ever permanently "wins" — but generation after generation, both get faster. GAN training works the same way, just over thousands of training steps instead of thousands of years.

cards.title: From blobs to believable faces
cards.body: Early in training, the generator's faces are indistinguishable blobs, and the discriminator catches every single one with ease. A thousand steps later, the generator's faces have rough eyes and a mouth — the discriminator has to look harder. By late training, the generator's faces can look convincingly real, and the discriminator has become a genuinely skilled judge of subtle flaws.

cards.title: It's not a one-time victory
cards.body: You might expect one network to "solve" the other and stay ahead forever. In practice, healthy GAN training is a constant back-and-forth: whichever network briefly falls behind gets pushed to catch up over the next few turns.

cards.title: Early training vs. later training
cards.body: How the quality bar shifts as both networks co-evolve:

exercises.prompt: As GAN training progresses in a healthy way, what happens to the discriminator's task?

options.text: It gets harder, because the fakes it sees keep getting more convincing
It gets easier with every single step
It stops being needed after a few steps
It turns into a regression problem

exercises.explain: Right — a co-evolving generator keeps raising the bar for the discriminator.

exercises.prompt: True or false: in a well-trained GAN, only the generator improves over time while the discriminator stays exactly the same.
exercises.explain: False — both networks improve together; that mutual pressure is the whole point of adversarial training.

exercises.sentence: A better discriminator forces a better generator, and a better generator forces a better ___.
exercises.explain: It's a two-way street — each network's improvement raises the bar for the other.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best reflects healthy co-improvement in GAN training?

options.text: Both the generator's fakes and the discriminator's judgment noticeably sharpen over thousands of steps
The generator improves for 10 steps, then training stops for good
The discriminator's accuracy stays frozen at 50% forever
The generator perfectly copies a training image by the second step

exercises.explain: That steady, mutual sharpening of both networks is exactly what healthy adversarial training looks like.

exercises.prompt: Over the course of training, both your generator and discriminator get noticeably better — but so far you've only described their improvement in plain language ("more convincing," "sharper judgment"). What do you actually need in order to turn "the discriminator was fooled" or "the discriminator caught it" into a number a neural network can learn from?

options.text: A loss function that mathematically scores how well each network is doing at its opposing goal, so gradients can flow back and update the weights
A larger dataset of real training images
A longer random noise vector fed to the generator
More hidden layers in the discriminator

exercises.explain: Exactly — next up, the adversarial loss function that turns this whole competitive game into numbers both networks can learn from.
